title: Why Big Companies Struggle to Negotiate Great Deals
content:
Enterprise negotiations often falter not because the negotiators lack skill, but because they are constrained by structural problems. Frontline negotiators are typically incentivized to close deals quickly, creating an inherent agency problem. Furthermore, they are bound by narrow mandates and preapproved terms dictated by headquarters, causing an alignment problem. Success requires a fundamental shift in how these corporate negotiators are managed and empowered. Dealmakers should be treated as problem-solvers without immediate commitment authority, allowing them to explore creative solutions.

This approach shifts the dynamic from a battle over rigid terms to a collaborative search for mutual value. Organizations must also decide which deals and individual issues are truly worth negotiating. Not every contract requires bespoke terms, and standardizing routine agreements can save immense amounts of time. For highly complex deals, integrating ongoing stakeholder engagement is critical to success. This is often more effective than relying on an early, forced consensus that limits flexibility later.

Replacing traditional deal review boards with proactive deal value boards can further improve outcomes. These modern boards focus on maximizing the overall value of the deal rather than just ensuring strict compliance. They actively assist negotiators in identifying creative trade-offs that benefit both parties. Done well, this approach results in shortened deal cycles and greater leverage in complex situations. Ultimately, it produces more valuable and sustainable outcomes for the entire enterprise.
